label: Industrial Revolution
pitch:
The Spinning Jenny invented in 1764 was just the spark — when Watt improves the steam engine five years later, the entire trajectory of human civilization bent. By the time the Liverpool & Manchester Railway opened in 1830, the world had been remade: factories replaced workshops, cities swallowed the countryside, and the Great Exhibition at Crystal Palace celebrated an age of machines.

🔑 Key Facts:
• Watt's separate condenser made steam power efficient enough to drive the whole economy
• The railway annihilated distance and forced the world onto a single clock
• Bessemer steel and electricity launched a Second Industrial Revolution after 1870
• The Communist Manifesto of 1848 was a direct response to the misery industrialization created

🤔 Why It Matters: Every modern convenience — mass-produced goods, electricity, global supply chains — descends from this era, as do the labor rights, inequality debates, and carbon emissions that define the present.

🎲 Wild Card: During the Luddite uprisings the British government deployed more soldiers against machine-breaking textile workers than it had fighting Napoleon in Spain.

spans.label: Preconditions & Proto-Industry
spans.description: An agricultural revolution, parliamentary enclosure, population growth, cheap coal, Atlantic colonial markets, and a uniquely commercial scientific culture assembled the conditions for industrialization in Britain before the first famous machines appeared.

spans.label: The First Industrial Revolution
spans.description: Mechanized cotton spinning, Watt's improved steam engine, coke iron, and the factory system transformed Britain into the world's first industrial economy, concentrating workers into mills and cities at unprecedented speed.

spans.label: The Railway Age & Workshop of the World
spans.description: Steam railways annihilated distance, free trade triumphed over the landed interest, and the 1851 Great Exhibition crowned Britain the workshop of the world even as cholera, slums, and the standard-of-living debate exposed the human cost.

spans.label: The Second Industrial Revolution
spans.description: Cheap Bessemer steel, the chemical industry, electricity, the internal combustion engine, and mass production launched a science-based second wave that let Germany and the United States overtake Britain and pulled Japan into the industrial world.

spans.label: The Social Question
spans.description: Machine-breaking, massacres, trade unions, Chartism, factory legislation, public-health reform, and the rival ideologies of liberalism and socialism formed a century-long argument about who industrial wealth was for.

events.label: Newcomen's atmospheric engine
events.description: Thomas Newcomen built the first commercially successful steam engine, an atmospheric beam engine used to pump water out of coal and tin mines. Crude and fuel-hungry, it nonetheless ran for decades and proved that steam could do heavy mechanical work.

events.label: Darby smelts iron with coke
events.description: Abraham Darby succeeded in smelting iron using coke instead of scarce charcoal at Coalbrookdale. The breakthrough freed ironmaking from dependence on dwindling forests and tied it permanently to coalfields.

events.label: The British Agricultural Revolution
events.description: Crop rotation, selective livestock breeding, new fodder crops, and consolidated farms roughly doubled English agricultural output over the century. The surplus fed a growing non-farming population and freed labor for industry.

events.label: 'Turnip' Townshend popularizes crop rotation
events.description: Charles Townshend promoted the four-field Norfolk rotation using turnips and clover to restore soil and feed winter livestock. The technique eliminated fallow fields and raised yields without new land.

events.label: Jethro Tull's seed drill
events.description: Jethro Tull devised a horse-drawn seed drill that sowed seed in neat rows at controlled depth, replacing wasteful broadcasting by hand. It was an early sign that mechanization could be applied to age-old tasks.

events.label: Robert Bakewell's selective breeding
events.description: Robert Bakewell systematized livestock breeding, transforming scrawny medieval sheep and cattle into fast-fattening modern meat animals. His methods spread the principle that biology, like machinery, could be deliberately improved.

events.label: Parliamentary Enclosure Acts accelerate
events.description: Thousands of Acts of Parliament privatized open fields and common land, consolidating them into fenced private farms. The process raised farm productivity but stripped rural laborers of customary rights and pushed many toward the towns.

events.label: London's financial system matures
events.description: The Bank of England, a funded national debt, marine insurance at Lloyd's, and a stock market gave Britain the deepest capital markets in the world. Cheap, reliable credit later financed canals, factories, and railways.

events.label: The Atlantic trade system
events.description: Slave-grown sugar, tobacco, and cotton, and protected colonial markets for British manufactures, generated profits and demand that fed early industry. The plantation economy was inseparable from the cotton-mill economy that followed.

events.label: Kay's flying shuttle
events.description: John Kay's flying shuttle let a single weaver throw the shuttle across a wide loom mechanically, doubling weaving speed. By outpacing hand spinners it created the yarn shortage that the spinning inventions would race to solve.

events.label: Coalbrookdale becomes an iron center
events.description: The Darby family's works in the Severn Gorge pioneered coke iron, iron rails, iron wheels, and the first iron cylinders for steam engines. Coalbrookdale was the cradle of the iron-and-coal economy.

events.label: Society of Arts founded
events.description: The Society for the Encouragement of Arts, Manufactures and Commerce offered prizes for useful inventions and improvements. It embodied the Enlightenment conviction that applied knowledge should be rewarded and diffused.

events.label: Britain's patent system
events.description: An imperfect but functioning patent system let inventors claim temporary monopolies on new machines, turning ingenuity into property. Arkwright, Watt, and others built fortunes and lawsuits on their patents.

events.label: Bridgewater Canal opens
events.description: The Duke of Bridgewater's canal, engineered by James Brindley, carried coal from Worsley to Manchester and halved its price. Its commercial success triggered Britain's canal-building boom.

events.label: Spinning Jenny invented
events.description: James Hargreaves invented the spinning jenny, a multi-spindle frame that let one worker spin many threads at once. It was one of the key innovations that launched the textile revolution.

events.label: Watt conceives the separate condenser
events.description: Repairing a model Newcomen engine, James Watt realized that condensing steam in a separate vessel would stop the catastrophic waste of reheating the cylinder. The insight was the conceptual core of the modern steam engine.

events.label: Arkwright's water frame
events.description: Richard Arkwright patented the water frame, a water-powered spinning machine that produced thread strong enough to be used as warp. Its scale demanded a purpose-built powered building rather than a cottage.

events.label: Watt patents the improved steam engine
events.description: James Watt patented his separate condenser, making the steam engine far more fuel-efficient. His later partnership with Matthew Boulton brought reliable steam power to factories, mines, and transport.

events.label: Cromford Mill opens
events.description: Arkwright opened his water-powered cotton mill at Cromford, establishing the template for the modern factory. Hundreds of workers, including children, labored in shifts around the clock under strict discipline.

events.label: Wedgwood's Etruria pottery works
events.description: Josiah Wedgwood opened a factory that applied division of labor, branding, and clocked discipline to pottery. He pioneered modern marketing and showed that the factory method extended beyond textiles.

events.label: The Lunar Society of Birmingham
events.description: Boulton, Watt, Wedgwood, Priestley, and Darwin met monthly to fuse science, invention, and enterprise. The club embodied the Industrial Revolution's distinctive marriage of natural philosophy and business.

events.label: Arkwright builds the factory system
events.description: Richard Arkwright integrated machines, water power, waged shift labor, and managerial discipline into a reproducible industrial model. Contemporaries called him the father of the factory system.

events.label: Adam Smith's Wealth of Nations
events.description: Adam Smith argued that the division of labor and self-interested exchange in free markets generate national prosperity. The book gave industrial capitalism its founding theory and its enduring vocabulary.

events.label: Boulton & Watt partnership
events.description: Matthew Boulton's capital and salesmanship turned Watt's patent into a manufacturing business at the Soho works in Birmingham. The firm became the prototype of the science-based engineering company.

events.label: Iron Bridge at Coalbrookdale
events.description: The world's first major cast-iron bridge spanned the River Severn at Coalbrookdale. It advertised the structural promise of iron and became the emblem of the new industrial age.

events.label: Crompton's spinning mule
events.description: Samuel Crompton combined the jenny and water frame into the spinning mule, producing fine yet strong yarn. It became the dominant spinning technology for a century and made Britain's finest cotton possible.

events.label: Watt's rotary engine
events.description: Watt patented gearing that converted the engine's reciprocating stroke into rotary motion. Steam could now turn shafts, freeing factories from rivers and powering machinery anywhere.

events.label: Cort's puddling process for iron
events.description: Henry Cort patented puddling and rolling, producing wrought iron in bulk using coal rather than charcoal. British bar-iron output soared, supplying machines, rails, and bridges.

events.label: Cartwright's power loom
events.description: Edmund Cartwright patented the power loom, mechanizing weaving as spinning had already been mechanized. Slow to perfect, it eventually destroyed the handloom weavers' trade.

events.label: Steam-powered cotton mills
events.description: Cotton spinners began installing Boulton & Watt rotative engines, the first factories driven by steam rather than water. The energy ceiling on industrial growth was lifted.

events.label: Mechanized wool and flax
events.description: Inventors extended powered spinning and combing from cotton to wool, flax, and worsted. Mechanization spread across the entire textile sector within a generation.

events.label: Whitney's cotton gin
events.description: Eli Whitney's gin separated short-staple cotton fiber from seed fifty times faster than by hand. It made the American South the world's cotton supplier and entrenched and expanded slavery there.

events.label: Canal Mania
events.description: A speculative boom built a dense network of inland waterways linking coalfields, ports, and factory towns. Canals slashed bulk-freight costs and were the circulatory system of early industrialization.

events.label: Manchester becomes 'Cottonopolis'
events.description: Manchester exploded from a market town into the world capital of cotton manufacturing and the first true industrial city. Its unplanned growth produced both immense wealth and notorious squalor.

events.label: Methodist revival in industrial towns
events.description: John Wesley's Methodism spread rapidly among industrial workers, offering community, literacy, discipline, and dignity. It became the characteristic faith of much of the English working class.

events.label: Sunday School movement
events.description: Robert Raikes promoted Sunday Schools to give working children basic literacy on their one free day. The movement taught millions and was one of the few social services open to the poor.

events.label: Society for Abolition of the Slave Trade
events.description: Granville Sharp, Thomas Clarkson, and Quaker activists founded the first mass abolitionist campaign, using pamphlets, petitions, and a famous logo. It pioneered modern moral-pressure politics.

events.label: Paine's Rights of Man
events.description: Thomas Paine's defense of the French Revolution sold in unprecedented numbers among artisans and workers. It put democratic and egalitarian ideas into the hands of the people industry was creating.

events.label: Wollstonecraft's Vindication
events.description: Mary Wollstonecraft argued for women's education and equality as the factory began drawing women into waged labor outside the home. Her work seeded a debate industrial society could not avoid.

events.label: Combination Acts ban unions
events.description: Parliament outlawed combinations of workers to raise wages or shorten hours, criminalizing early trade unionism. The law drove labor organization underground for a generation.

events.label: Malthus' Essay on Population
events.description: Thomas Malthus argued that population grows geometrically while food grows arithmetically, dooming the poor to subsistence crises. His pessimism shaped economics, Poor Law policy, and later Darwin.

events.label: Jenner develops smallpox vaccine
events.description: Edward Jenner showed that cowpox inoculation protected against smallpox. His discovery founded immunology and began the long decline of an ancient killer.

events.label: Maudslay's screw-cutting lathe
events.description: Henry Maudslay built a precision lathe that cut accurate, standardized screw threads. Precision machine tools made interchangeable parts and mass production conceivable.

events.label: Portsmouth Block Mills
events.description: Marc Brunel and Maudslay installed 45 machines that let ten unskilled men out-produce 110 skilled craftsmen making ships' pulley blocks. It was arguably the first mass-production line.

events.label: Trevithick's steam locomotive
events.description: Richard Trevithick ran the first steam locomotive to haul a load on rails, at the Penydarren ironworks in Wales. He proved high-pressure steam traction was possible decades before it was commercial.

events.label: Robert Owen's New Lanark
events.description: Robert Owen ran the New Lanark mills with decent housing, schools, and shorter hours while still turning a profit. He argued that environment, not vice, produced misery, founding industrial paternalism and socialism.

events.label: Abolition of the slave trade
events.description: Parliament banned the British transatlantic slave trade after a twenty-year campaign led by Wilberforce and Clarkson. Slave-grown cotton still fed the mills, making the act a partial moral reckoning.

events.label: Gas street lighting in London
events.description: Pall Mall became the first street lit by coal gas, and the Gas Light and Coke Company followed in 1812. Artificial light lengthened the working day and reshaped urban life.

events.label: Ricardo's Principles of Political Economy
events.description: David Ricardo formalized rent, comparative advantage, and the 'iron law of wages,' giving free trade and laissez-faire their rigorous theory. His system dominated economic policy for decades.

events.label: Luddite uprisings
events.description: Textile workers smashed the machines they blamed for unemployment and starvation wages across the Midlands and North. The government deployed more troops against them than against Napoleon in Spain.

events.label: Frame Breaking Act makes machine-breaking capital
events.description: Parliament made the destruction of textile machinery punishable by death, over Lord Byron's maiden-speech objection. Several Luddites were hanged or transported under it.

events.label: Davy safety lamp
events.description: Humphry Davy invented a wire-gauze lamp that prevented firedamp explosions in coal mines. It saved lives but also enabled deeper, more dangerous pits.

events.label: Stephenson's first locomotive
events.description: George Stephenson built the colliery locomotive Blücher and went on to standardize rail traction. A self-taught engineman, he became the central figure of the railway age.

events.label: Peterloo Massacre
events.description: Cavalry charged a peaceful crowd of about 60,000 reform demonstrators at St Peter's Field, Manchester, killing roughly 18 and injuring hundreds. It became a permanent symbol of repression and a spur to reform.

events.label: The Six Acts
events.description: After Peterloo, Parliament passed repressive laws curbing public meetings, the radical press, and arms. The state met industrial discontent with censorship and force.

events.label: The Swing Riots
events.description: Farm labourers across southern England burned ricks and smashed the threshing machines that were destroying their winter work. Like the Luddites, they were met with hangings and transportation rather than relief.

events.label: Stockton & Darlington Railway
events.description: The world's first public railway to use steam locomotives opened in northeast England. It proved that steam rail could move freight commercially and ignited the railway revolution.

events.label: Combination Acts repealed
events.description: Parliament repealed the Combination Acts, legalizing trade unions for the first time. Workers could now organize openly, though strikes remained legally hazardous.

events.label: Rainhill Trials
events.description: A locomotive competition chose the engine for the Liverpool & Manchester line; Stephenson's Rocket won decisively. It fixed the design template for the steam locomotive.

events.label: Liverpool & Manchester Railway
events.description: The first modern inter-city railway carried passengers and freight on a timetable between two industrial cities. Its opening, marred by the first railway fatality, announced the transport age.

events.label: Faraday discovers electromagnetic induction
events.description: Michael Faraday showed that a moving magnet induces an electric current, the principle behind every generator and electric motor. It was the seed of the later electrical industry.

events.label: Great Reform Act
events.description: The Reform Act enfranchised much of the industrial middle class and abolished 'rotten boroughs,' redistributing seats toward the new cities. It was a political adjustment to industrial reality, but excluded the working class.

events.label: Slavery abolished in the British Empire
events.description: Parliament abolished slavery across most of the empire, compensating slave-owners rather than the enslaved. Emancipation reshaped the cotton economy and the moral self-image of industrial Britain.

events.label: Factory Act of 1833
events.description: Parliament barred factory work by children under nine, limited older children's hours, and created the first paid factory inspectors. It was the first enforceable regulation of industrial labor.

events.label: New Poor Law
events.description: The Poor Law Amendment Act abolished outdoor relief for the able-bodied and forced paupers into deliberately harsh workhouses. The deterrent system became a hated symbol of industrial-era cruelty.

events.label: Tolpuddle Martyrs transported
events.description: Six Dorset farm laborers were transported to Australia for swearing a union oath. The harsh sentence provoked mass protest and became a founding legend of the labor movement.

events.label: Owen's Grand National union
events.description: Robert Owen's Grand National Consolidated Trades Union briefly enrolled hundreds of thousands in a bid for one big union. Its rapid collapse showed how fragile early mass unionism was.

events.label: Municipal Corporations Act
events.description: The Act created elected town councils, giving the new industrial cities a modern, accountable form of government. Local sanitary and policing reform later flowed from it.

events.label: Cooke and Wheatstone telegraph
events.description: William Cooke and Charles Wheatstone patented the first commercial electric telegraph, deployed along railway lines. For the first time information moved faster than a messenger.

events.label: Morse's telegraph and code
events.description: Samuel Morse demonstrated a single-wire telegraph and the dot-dash code that bore his name. The 1844 Washington–Baltimore line proved instant continental communication.

events.label: Chartist movement
events.description: Working-class radicals demanded the six points of the People's Charter, including universal male suffrage and the secret ballot. The first mass political movement of industrial workers, it failed then but five of its six demands later became law.

events.label: The People's Charter published
events.description: The Charter set out six democratic demands drafted by London artisans. It became the program of the largest working-class campaign of the century.

events.label: Rowland Hill's Penny Post
events.description: Uniform penny postage and the adhesive stamp made cheap nationwide letter-writing universal. Communication, like travel, was being democratized by industrial logistics.

events.label: Dickens' Oliver Twist
events.description: Charles Dickens dramatized workhouses, child labor, and slum crime for a mass readership. His serialized novels turned the 'condition of England' into popular conscience.

events.label: Carlyle names the 'Condition of England Question'
events.description: Thomas Carlyle's pamphlet Chartism asked why industrial wealth coincided with mass misery. The phrase framed a debate that dominated Victorian writing.

events.label: Mines and Collieries Act
events.description: After a shocking royal commission report, Parliament barred women and children under ten from underground mine work. State investigation of industrial conditions was becoming routine.

events.label: Anti-Corn Law League
events.description: Cobden and Bright built a mass middle-class campaign against the protectionist grain tariff. Its disciplined organizing pioneered modern single-issue pressure politics.

events.label: Repeal of the Corn Laws
events.description: Peel repealed the protectionist grain tariffs amid the Irish famine, committing Britain to free trade. The vote marked the political triumph of industry over the landed aristocracy.

events.label: The Great Irish Famine
events.description: Potato blight and laissez-faire relief policy killed about a million people and drove a million more to emigrate. It was a catastrophe shaped by industrial-age economic ideology.

events.label: Engels' Condition of the Working Class
events.description: Friedrich Engels, managing his family's Manchester mill, documented the city's slums, disease, and exploitation in damning detail. The book gave industrial misery its classic eyewitness indictment.

events.label: Ten Hours Act
events.description: Parliament limited women's and young persons' textile-factory work to ten hours a day, the campaign's signal victory. It established the principle that the state could cap the working day.

events.label: Communist Manifesto published
events.description: Marx and Engels analyzed industrial capitalism as a revolutionary, self-undermining system and called workers to overthrow it. It became the most influential political pamphlet in modern history.

events.label: The Revolutions of 1848
events.description: Liberal and nationalist revolts swept Continental Europe as industrial dislocation met political grievance. Their failure scattered radicals like Marx into exile in industrial Britain.

events.label: Chartism's last great petition
events.description: A mass Chartist rally on Kennington Common and a giant petition failed to move Parliament, and the movement faded. Its democratic program would nonetheless be enacted piecemeal over decades.

events.label: Chadwick's Sanitary Report
events.description: Edwin Chadwick's report linked filth, bad water, and overcrowding to disease and early death in industrial cities. It launched the public-health movement.

events.label: Public Health Act 1848
events.description: Spurred by cholera, Parliament created a General Board of Health and let towns build sewers and water supplies. The state began accepting responsibility for the urban environment industry had made.

events.label: The cholera epidemics
events.description: Repeated cholera waves killed tens of thousands in the unsewered industrial cities. The disease's terror finally forced sanitary reform.

events.label: Great Exhibition at the Crystal Palace
events.description: The first world's fair displayed global industry under Paxton's vast iron-and-glass Crystal Palace. Six million visitors witnessed Britain crowned the workshop of the world.

events.label: Paxton's Crystal Palace
events.description: Joseph Paxton's prefabricated glass-and-iron hall, raised in months from standardized parts, was itself the Exhibition's greatest exhibit. It announced a new architecture of mass-produced components.

events.label: Singer's sewing machine
events.description: Isaac Singer's practical sewing machine, sold on installment credit, became one of the first mass-marketed consumer durables. It mechanized the home and the garment sweatshop alike.

events.label: Slater's Mill in Rhode Island
events.description: Samuel Slater rebuilt Arkwright's machinery from memory after emigrating, founding the American factory system. Britain's industrial secrets had crossed the Atlantic in a single man's head.

events.label: The Lowell mill system
events.description: Boston capitalists built integrated cotton towns staffed by young farm women living in supervised boarding houses. The Lowell system was America's distinctive answer to the factory question.

events.label: The American System of Manufactures
events.description: U.S. armories and clockmakers pursued interchangeable parts and machine production of standardized goods. The approach later underpinned mass production and the assembly line.

events.label: The Erie Canal opens
events.description: The Erie Canal linked the Great Lakes to the Atlantic, opening the American interior to commerce. It showed that the transport revolution would be continental in scale in the New World.

events.label: British technology crosses the Channel
events.description: Emigrant engineers like William Cockerill carried textile and engine technology to Belgium, France, and the German states despite British export bans. Industrialization began to leak out of its birthplace.

events.label: Cockerill's works at Seraing
events.description: John Cockerill built an integrated coal, iron, and engineering complex near Liège, making Belgium the first industrialized nation after Britain. Continental industry had its first giant.

events.label: The German Zollverein
events.description: A Prussian-led customs union created a single tariff-free German market years before political unification. The common market was a precondition of Germany's later industrial surge.

events.label: List's National System of Political Economy
events.description: Friedrich List argued that latecomers must protect 'infant industries' rather than embrace free trade. His doctrine guided German, American, and later Japanese industrial policy.

events.label: Krupp's Essen steelworks
events.description: The Krupp firm grew from a small Ruhr foundry into Europe's largest steel and armaments maker. It symbolized the fusion of heavy industry, the state, and war in industrializing Germany.

events.label: Railway Mania
events.description: A speculative frenzy authorized thousands of miles of British railway and ended in a crash that ruined many investors. The bubble nonetheless left behind a near-complete rail network.

events.label: Railway time standardizes the clock
events.description: Railways forced towns to abandon local solar time for a single national 'railway time' so timetables could work. Industrial logistics, not astronomy, put the world on standard time.

events.label: Brunel's Great Western Railway
events.description: Isambard Kingdom Brunel engineered the broad-gauge Great Western with audacious bridges, tunnels, and the steamship that connected it to New York. He embodied the heroic age of engineering.

events.label: SS Great Britain launched
events.description: Brunel's iron-hulled, screw-propelled steamship was the first to combine both technologies at large scale. It pointed toward the steel ocean liner and the shrinking of the world.

events.label: The Bessemer process
events.description: Henry Bessemer's converter blew air through molten iron to make steel cheaply and in bulk for the first time. Abundant steel rebuilt railways, ships, and cities and opened the Second Industrial Revolution.

events.label: The Siemens–Martin open-hearth process
events.description: The open-hearth furnace produced higher-quality steel from scrap and pig iron and eventually overtook the Bessemer converter. It made steel the universal structural material.

events.label: Perkin's mauveine launches synthetic dyes
events.description: William Perkin accidentally synthesized the first aniline dye from coal tar. Synthetic chemistry became a science-based industry that Germany would soon dominate.

events.label: The Solvay soda process
events.description: Ernest Solvay's ammonia-soda process gave the chemical industry cheap alkali for soap, glass, and textiles. Heavy chemicals became a pillar of the second industrial wave.

events.label: BASF, Bayer, and the German chemical giants
events.description: Firms like BASF and Bayer built industrial research laboratories that turned chemistry into a deliberate engine of products and profit. Science-based industry was born in the German dye works.

events.label: Darwin's On the Origin of Species
events.description: Charles Darwin's theory of evolution by natural selection, partly inspired by Malthus, reframed nature, religion, and the idea of progress. It was the era's deepest intellectual shock.

events.label: Mill's On Liberty
events.description: John Stuart Mill defended individual freedom against mass society and majority tyranny. His liberalism tried to humanize the competitive order industry had created.

events.label: Mill's Principles of Political Economy
events.description: John Stuart Mill's textbook accepted markets but argued distribution was a human choice, opening classical economics toward reform. It dominated economic teaching for a generation.

events.label: Mayhew's London Labour and the London Poor
events.description: Henry Mayhew's interview-based survey of street people gave the urban poor a documented voice. It pioneered social investigation as a literary form.

events.label: Gaskell's industrial novels
events.description: Elizabeth Gaskell's Mary Barton and North and South dramatized the masters-and-hands conflict from inside Manchester. The 'industrial novel' became a moral genre of its own.

events.label: Dickens' Hard Times
events.description: Set in the fictional Coketown, Hard Times satirized utilitarian education and factory-town life. It was Dickens' most direct attack on industrial ideology.

events.label: Ruskin's Unto This Last
events.description: John Ruskin attacked the moral foundations of political economy and insisted wealth meant human flourishing, not accumulation. His essays shaped later socialism and conservation alike.

events.label: The Civil War and the cotton famine
events.description: The Union blockade of the Confederacy cut off raw cotton and idled Lancashire's mills, throwing hundreds of thousands out of work. The crisis exposed how dependent industrial Britain was on slave-grown cotton.

events.label: America's wartime industrial takeoff
events.description: The Civil War accelerated Northern railroads, ironworks, and mass production of standardized goods. The Union's victory was partly an industrial one, and it set the United States on the path to becoming the leading industrial power.

events.label: The London Underground opens
events.description: The world's first underground railway began carrying passengers beneath the congested capital. The industrial city was now being engineered in three dimensions.

events.label: The First International founded
events.description: The International Workingmen's Association, with Marx as its leading voice, tried to unite labor across borders. It marked the emergence of an organized international socialist movement.

events.label: Second Reform Act
events.description: Disraeli's Act roughly doubled the electorate by enfranchising many urban working men. The industrial working class was being cautiously admitted to the political nation.

events.label: Marx's Das Kapital, Volume I
events.description: Karl Marx's analysis of capital, surplus value, and crisis offered the deepest critique of the industrial system ever written. It became the theoretical foundation of the world socialist movement.

events.label: The Suez Canal opens
events.description: The Suez Canal cut weeks off the Europe–Asia sea route, binding the industrial core to its colonial sources and markets. Steam shipping and the canal compressed the globe.

events.label: The U.S. transcontinental railroad
events.description: The golden spike at Promontory Summit joined the American coasts by rail. Industrial transport had bound a continent and signaled the rise of a new industrial superpower.

events.label: The Meiji Restoration
events.description: Japan's new Meiji government launched a state-driven crash program of industrialization to avoid colonization. It was the first successful non-Western industrial revolution.

events.label: Japan's model factories and zaibatsu
events.description: The Meiji state built model railways, mills, and shipyards, then sold them to family conglomerates, the zaibatsu. Japan compressed Britain's century of industrialization into a few decades.

events.label: The deindustrialization of India
events.description: Cheap Lancashire cloth and colonial policy destroyed India's vast handloom textile industry, turning a manufacturing region into a raw-cotton supplier. Industrialization in Britain meant deindustrialization in the colonies.

events.label: The Great Divergence
events.description: Industrialization opened an enormous and lasting gap in wealth and power between the West and the rest of the world. Why it happened where and when it did remains one of history's central debates.

events.label: The Opium Wars and China
events.description: Britain forced opium and free trade on China at gunpowder point to balance its tea purchases. Industrial-era 'free trade' was backed by industrial-era gunboats.

events.label: Otto's four-stroke engine
events.description: Nikolaus Otto built the first practical four-stroke internal combustion engine. Compact power independent of steam boilers opened the road to the automobile.

events.label: Bell patents the telephone
events.description: Alexander Graham Bell patented a device that carried the human voice over wires. Communication moved from coded signals to live speech.

events.label: Edison's incandescent lighting system
events.description: Thomas Edison demonstrated a durable incandescent bulb and, more importantly, the generating and distribution system to power it. Electricity became a sellable commodity.

events.label: Pearl Street power station
events.description: Edison's Manhattan station delivered electricity commercially to paying customers for the first time. The electrical utility industry was born.

events.label: Tesla's alternating-current system
events.description: Nikola Tesla's AC induction motor and polyphase system, backed by Westinghouse, allowed power to be transmitted over long distances. AC won the 'war of the currents' and electrified the world.

events.label: Benz builds the first automobile
events.description: Karl Benz patented a practical petrol-engined motor car, and Daimler followed independently. Personal mechanical transport had arrived.

events.label: Diesel's compression engine
events.description: Rudolf Diesel patented a high-efficiency compression-ignition engine. It would later power ships, locomotives, and industry worldwide.

events.label: Parsons' steam turbine
events.description: Charles Parsons invented the steam turbine, which would generate most of the world's electricity and drive its fastest ships. It was the steam engine's high-efficiency successor.

events.label: The transatlantic telegraph cable
events.description: A working submarine cable linked Europe and North America, cutting transatlantic message time from days to minutes. Global finance and news became near-instantaneous.

events.label: Pasteur and germ theory
events.description: Louis Pasteur's experiments established that microbes cause fermentation and disease. Germ theory transformed medicine, brewing, and public health in the crowded industrial city.

events.label: John Snow traces cholera to water
events.description: Physician John Snow mapped a London outbreak to a single contaminated water pump, founding modern epidemiology. Industrial-city disease was finally yielding to scientific analysis.

events.label: Bazalgette's London sewers
events.description: After the 'Great Stink' of 1858, Joseph Bazalgette built a vast intercepting sewer system that ended London's cholera. Civil engineering on an industrial scale made the giant city survivable.

events.label: Haussmann rebuilds Paris
events.description: Baron Haussmann drove boulevards, sewers, and water mains through medieval Paris. The industrial age was now remaking the form of the great cities themselves.

events.label: Le Bon Marché and the department store
events.description: Paris's Bon Marché pioneered fixed prices, vast inventories, and shopping as spectacle. Mass production was met by mass retailing and the birth of the consumer.

events.label: Limited Liability Act
events.description: Parliament let investors risk only their stake, not their whole fortune, in a company. Limited liability unleashed the modern joint-stock corporation.

events.label: Carnegie's American steel empire
events.description: Andrew Carnegie applied Bessemer steel and ruthless cost-cutting to build the world's largest steel enterprise. The modern vertically integrated corporation was taking shape in America.

events.label: Rockefeller's Standard Oil trust
events.description: John D. Rockefeller's Standard Oil controlled most American refining through the trust device. Industrial capitalism had produced monopoly and the politics of antitrust.

events.label: The Second Industrial Revolution
events.description: Steel, electricity, chemicals, oil, and the internal combustion engine drove a second, science-based industrial wave centered on Germany and the United States. Britain's early lead was overtaken.

events.label: Germany overtakes Britain in heavy industry
events.description: By the 1900s German steel, chemical, and electrical output surpassed Britain's. The first industrial nation had been overtaken by a science-organized rival.

events.label: The United States becomes the largest economy
events.description: Around 1890 American industrial output surpassed Britain's, making the U.S. the world's largest economy. Scale, resources, and mass production had shifted the industrial center westward.

events.label: Trade Union Act 1871
events.description: Parliament gave trade unions full legal status and protected their funds. Organized labor became a permanent, lawful institution of industrial society.

events.label: The Paris Commune
events.description: Parisian workers seized the city for two months before a bloody suppression killed thousands. The Commune became a martyr-legend and a warning that haunted the propertied classes.

events.label: Forster's Education Act
events.description: The Act began state-funded elementary schooling in England and Wales. An industrial economy now needed, and feared, a literate workforce.

events.label: The Long Depression
events.description: Falling prices, agricultural collapse, and slow growth spread doubt about laissez-faire across the industrial world. It pushed nations back toward tariffs, empire, and state action.

events.label: New Unionism and the Dock Strike
events.description: The London dock strike showed unskilled workers could organize and win, launching mass 'new unionism.' The industrial working class had become an organized social force.

events.label: The Haymarket affair
events.description: A Chicago labor rally for the eight-hour day ended in a bomb, a riot, and contested executions. American industrial conflict turned violent and gave the world May Day.

events.label: The Second International
events.description: Socialist and labor parties founded a new international, institutionalizing May Day and the eight-hour demand. Mass socialist politics was now a permanent feature of industrial states.

events.label: Bismarck's social insurance
events.description: Germany introduced state health, accident, and old-age insurance to blunt socialism's appeal. The industrial welfare state was invented from above by a conservative.

events.label: Rerum Novarum
events.description: Pope Leo XIII's encyclical condemned both unrestrained capitalism and socialism and affirmed workers' rights and a just wage. Organized religion entered the industrial 'social question' directly.

events.label: The Salvation Army founded
events.description: William and Catherine Booth built a uniformed evangelical movement to reach the urban poor with food, shelter, and revival. Industrial misery generated new forms of organized religion.

events.label: The Social Gospel movement
events.description: Protestant reformers in Britain and America argued Christianity demanded justice for industrial workers, not just personal salvation. Faith became a force for labor and housing reform.

events.label: The Victorian crisis of faith
events.description: Geology, Darwin, biblical criticism, and industrial life eroded confident religious belief among the educated. Doubt itself became a defining Victorian experience.

events.label: Booth's survey of London poverty
events.description: Charles Booth's street-by-street poverty maps showed roughly a third of Londoners living in want. Statistical social science turned the 'condition of England' into measurable fact.

events.label: The Fabian Society
events.description: Middle-class intellectuals founded the Fabian Society to pursue socialism by gradual reform rather than revolution. It helped shape the future British Labour Party.

events.label: William Morris and the Arts and Crafts movement
events.description: William Morris rejected machine production for handcraft and a socialist vision of meaningful work. The Arts and Crafts movement was a romantic revolt against the factory.

events.label: Mass migration and the Statue of Liberty
events.description: Tens of millions left Europe for the industrial New World; the Statue of Liberty rose over their gateway. Industrialization set off the largest voluntary migration in history.

events.label: The international gold standard
events.description: Major economies pegged their currencies to gold, creating a stable framework for global trade and investment. Industrial 'first globalization' ran on the gold standard until 1914.

events.label: Refrigerated steamships
events.description: Refrigerated ships first carried frozen meat from the Southern Hemisphere to Europe. The industrial food chain went global, undercutting European agriculture.

events.label: Railways and steamships girdle the empire
events.description: British capital laid tens of thousands of miles of railway in India and across the colonies to extract raw materials and move troops. Industrial transport was an instrument of empire.

events.label: The Scramble for Africa
events.description: Industrial powers partitioned Africa for raw materials, markets, and prestige, enabled by steamships, quinine, the telegraph, and machine guns. Industrial technology made the new imperialism possible.

events.label: The Maxim gun
events.description: Hiram Maxim built the first automatic machine gun, giving small industrial armies overwhelming firepower over non-industrial peoples. Industry had transformed the balance of military power.

events.label: Coal becomes the world's engine
events.description: World coal output multiplied many times over as steam, steel, gas, and electricity all ran on it. The fossil-fuel economy and its carbon legacy were set in this century.

events.label: The carbon legacy begins
events.description: Industrial coal burning began measurably raising atmospheric carbon dioxide, a process Arrhenius first calculated in 1896. The Industrial Revolution opened the era now called the Anthropocene.

events.label: Arrhenius predicts global warming
events.description: Svante Arrhenius calculated that burning fossil fuel would warm the planet by raising carbon dioxide. The industrial age had produced both the cause and the first warning.

events.label: Taylor's scientific management
events.description: Frederick Taylor broke work into timed, measured tasks to maximize output. 'Taylorism' carried factory rationalization to its logical and dehumanizing extreme.

events.label: Toynbee names 'the Industrial Revolution'
events.description: Arnold Toynbee's posthumously published Oxford lectures popularized the term 'Industrial Revolution' in English. The age had begun to historicize and debate itself.

events.label: Living standards finally rise
events.description: After decades of stagnant or falling well-being, real wages, height, and life expectancy began clearly improving in the later nineteenth century. Whether industrialization helped or hurt workers before then is still debated.

events.label: Britain becomes a majority-urban nation
events.description: The 1851 census showed for the first time that most Britons lived in towns rather than the countryside. No large society had ever been urban before; industrialization made it normal.

events.label: George III
events.description: George III reigned through early industrialization, the loss of America, and the Napoleonic Wars. His long reign frames the First Industrial Revolution.

events.label: The reign of Queen Victoria
events.description: Victoria's long reign became synonymous with the industrial, imperial, and reforming age that bore her name. 'Victorian' is the era's shorthand.

events.label: The Napoleonic Wars and war industry
events.description: Two decades of war drove demand for iron, uniforms, and weapons and protected British industry behind the Royal Navy. War accelerated as well as disrupted industrialization.

events.label: Interchangeable parts in arms manufacture
events.description: U.S. armories pursued Whitney's and others' goal of interchangeable musket parts made by machine. The principle became the foundation of all later mass production.

events.label: Stephenson's Rocket
events.description: The Rocket combined a multi-tube boiler, blast pipe, and direct drive into the template for the modern locomotive. Every steam locomotive after it was a refinement of its design.

events.label: Precision engineering and the machine-tool trade
events.description: Bramah, Maudslay, Whitworth, and Nasmyth built the lathes, planers, and standards that let machines make machines. The machine-tool industry was the hidden multiplier of all the rest.

events.label: Whitworth's standard screw thread
events.description: Joseph Whitworth proposed the first national standard for screw threads. Standardization, dull but decisive, made interchangeable industrial parts possible across firms.

events.label: Nasmyth's steam hammer
events.description: James Nasmyth's steam hammer could forge huge components with controllable force. Heavy engineering now had a tool to match its ambitions.

events.label: The shift from iron to steel
events.description: Cheap bulk steel displaced wrought and cast iron in rails, ships, and structures within a generation. The material signature of industry changed from iron to steel.

events.label: The Eiffel Tower
events.description: Built for the 1889 Paris Exposition, the 1,000-foot iron tower flaunted what industrial metallurgy could do. It was the era's exclamation point in wrought iron.

events.label: The Brooklyn Bridge
events.description: The Roeblings' steel-wire suspension bridge spanned the East River on a scale previously unthinkable. It showed steel cable reshaping the possible.

events.label: The first skyscrapers
events.description: Chicago's steel-frame Home Insurance Building let cities grow upward instead of outward. Steel and the elevator created the vertical industrial metropolis.

events.label: The typewriter and the modern office
events.description: The commercial typewriter mechanized paperwork and drew women into clerical employment. Industrial method reached the office and the white-collar workforce.

events.label: Canning and the industrial diet
events.description: Tinned food, then factory bread, margarine, and condensed milk industrialized what people ate. Diet, like work, was being reorganized around the factory.

events.label: Mechanics' Institutes
events.description: Self-improvement institutes offered workers libraries and lectures in applied science. Industrial society generated its own adult-education movement.

events.label: The Rochdale Pioneers
events.description: Weavers in Rochdale founded a consumer cooperative whose rules became the model for the worldwide co-op movement. Workers built their own answer to the company shop.

events.label: The Sadler Report on child labor
events.description: Michael Sadler's parliamentary committee published harrowing testimony from child factory workers. The report's evidence drove the Factory Act of 1833.

events.label: Lord Shaftesbury's factory campaigns
events.description: The evangelical aristocrat Lord Shaftesbury led decades of campaigns against child labor in mines, mills, and chimneys. He embodied the religiously driven conscience of industrial reform.

events.label: Francis Place and artisan radicalism
events.description: The tailor Francis Place organized behind-the-scenes campaigns for union legalization and reform. Skilled artisans, not factory hands, led early working-class politics.

events.label: Feargus O'Connor and mass Chartism
events.description: The orator Feargus O'Connor and his paper the Northern Star turned Chartism into a national mass movement. Industrial workers had found a popular press and platform.

events.label: Cobden and Bright, apostles of free trade
events.description: Richard Cobden and John Bright preached free trade, peace, and retrenchment as a complete political creed. They were the purest political voice of the industrial middle class.

events.label: Thomas Brassey builds the world's railways
events.description: The contractor Thomas Brassey built railways on five continents with a mobile army of British navvies. Industrial Britain exported not just goods but the infrastructure itself.

events.label: The navvies
events.description: Hundreds of thousands of itinerant 'navvies' dug the canals and railways by pick and barrow at brutal human cost. The infrastructure of the machine age was built largely by muscle.

events.label: Why Britain? The Great Divergence debate
events.description: Historians from Pomeranz to Allen to Mokyr still argue whether coal, high wages, empire, science, or institutions made Britain first. The cause of the Industrial Revolution remains genuinely unsettled.

events.label: The horsepower defined
events.description: Watt coined 'horsepower' to sell engines by comparing them to the draft animals they replaced. The unit, and the mindset of measuring power, outlived the steam age.

events.label: Savery's 'Miner's Friend'
events.description: Thomas Savery patented an early steam pump for draining mines, the first commercial steam device. It was feeble and dangerous but proved the principle.

events.label: Deep coal mining
events.description: Steam pumps, the Davy lamp, and rising demand drove pits ever deeper and more dangerous. Coal was the literal foundation of every other industry.

events.label: The mill town
events.description: Purpose-built towns like Saltaire and the Lancashire cotton towns grew up around single industries. A new kind of human settlement, organized entirely around production, appeared.

events.label: Saltaire model village
events.description: Titus Salt built a clean model town around his mill, part paternalism, part advertisement. Industrial paternalism offered one answer to the slum.

events.label: The rise of the industrial bourgeoisie
events.description: Mill-owners, ironmasters, and bankers became the wealthiest and ultimately the most powerful class in Britain. Industrialization rewrote the social hierarchy from the top.

events.label: The servant economy
events.description: Domestic service was the largest single occupation for women in industrial Britain, larger than the mills. Industrial society ran on invisible household labor.

events.label: Women and children in the mills
events.description: Mill-owners preferred cheap, nimble women and children, who made up much of the early factory workforce. The factory reorganized the family as well as the economy.

events.label: The fall of the handloom weavers
events.description: Hundreds of thousands of once-prosperous handloom weavers were starved out by the power loom over a generation. They were the clearest human casualties of mechanization.

events.label: Reclaiming the workers' own history
events.description: E. P. Thompson's The Making of the English Working Class insisted the workers were agents, not victims, of industrialization. It reframed how the whole era is studied.

events.label: Samuel Smiles' Self-Help
events.description: Samuel Smiles' bestseller preached thrift, effort, and self-improvement as the road out of poverty. It crystallized Victorian middle-class faith in individual character.

events.label: The Clapham Sect and evangelical reform
events.description: Wilberforce's circle of wealthy evangelicals drove abolition and a wider campaign of moral reform. Industrial-era philanthropy had a powerful religious engine.

events.label: The temperance movement
events.description: Mass campaigns against drink blamed and tried to reform working-class life in the industrial city. Temperance was reform, social control, and self-respect at once.

events.label: Bentham's utilitarianism
events.description: Jeremy Bentham judged every law and institution by 'the greatest happiness of the greatest number.' Utilitarianism became the reforming philosophy of the industrial state.

events.label: Laissez-faire becomes orthodoxy
events.description: Smith's and Ricardo's followers hardened free-market economics into the near-religious doctrine of laissez-faire. It justified both prosperity and the refusal to relieve famine.

events.label: The Jacquard loom
events.description: Joseph-Marie Jacquard's punched-card loom wove complex patterns automatically. Its programmable cards directly inspired Babbage and the later computer.

events.label: Babbage's calculating engines
events.description: Charles Babbage designed mechanical computers and, with Ada Lovelace, the idea of programmable calculation. The information machine was conceived inside the machine age.

events.label: Photography invented
events.description: Daguerre and Talbot independently fixed images with chemistry and light. Industrial chemistry produced a new way of seeing and recording the world.

events.label: Vulcanized rubber
events.description: Charles Goodyear's vulcanization turned rubber into a stable industrial material. It became indispensable for seals, belts, tires, and insulation.

events.label: Anesthesia and antiseptic surgery
events.description: Ether anesthesia and Lister's antiseptic method made modern surgery possible. Industrial-age science began conquering pain and infection.

events.label: The laws of thermodynamics
events.description: Carnot, Joule, Kelvin, and Clausius worked out the science of heat and energy, partly by studying steam engines. For once, technology led and science followed.

events.label: Portland cement and reinforced concrete
events.description: Patented Portland cement, later combined with steel, gave builders a cheap, mouldable structural material. Modern construction's other basic material was an industrial product.

events.label: McCormick's mechanical reaper
events.description: Cyrus McCormick's horse-drawn reaper mechanized the grain harvest. Industry now reached back into agriculture, freeing still more rural labor.

events.label: Guano, nitrates, and industrial agriculture
events.description: Imported guano and Chilean nitrate fertilized European fields and fed industrial cities. Even farming had become an input to, and a customer of, global industry.

events.label: The investor and the stock exchange
events.description: Railways and limited liability created a mass class of shareholders and a culture of speculation, booms, and panics. Finance became central to industrial life.

events.label: The Panic of 1873
events.description: A railway-finance crash on both sides of the Atlantic opened the Long Depression. Industrial capitalism's boom-bust cycle was now a global phenomenon.

events.label: The Sherman Antitrust Act
events.description: The United States outlawed monopolistic combinations in restraint of trade. Industrial concentration had grown large enough to provoke the regulatory state.

events.label: The eight-hour day movement
events.description: Workers worldwide rallied around 'eight hours' work, eight hours' rest, eight hours' what we will.' The length of the working day became the century's defining labor demand.

events.label: The Trades Union Congress founded
events.description: British unions formed a national federation to lobby Parliament. Labor had become a permanent organized interest within the industrial state.

events.label: The Independent Labour Party
events.description: Keir Hardie and others founded a party to send working men to Parliament independently of the Liberals. Industrial labor was becoming a political party of its own.

events.label: The Married Women's Property Acts
events.description: Parliament let married women own their own wages and property for the first time. Industrial society slowly forced a rethinking of women's economic status.

events.label: Factory Act of 1844
events.description: New legislation required machine guards and tightened limits on women's and children's hours. Industrial safety regulation was becoming steadily more detailed.

events.label: Slum clearance and housing reform
events.description: Torrens and Cross Acts let towns demolish slums and build for the poor. The state slowly accepted responsibility for working-class housing.

events.label: Bournville and Port Sunlight
events.description: Quaker industrialists like the Cadburys and Levers built clean garden villages for their workers. Late-century paternalism prefigured the garden-city movement.

events.label: Disraeli's 'Two Nations'
events.description: Disraeli's novel Sybil described rich and poor as 'two nations between whom there is no intercourse.' The phrase named industrial Britain's central anxiety.

events.label: Blake's 'dark Satanic Mills'
events.description: William Blake's lines about England's 'dark Satanic Mills' gave the age its most enduring image of itself. Romantic revulsion at industry began as early as industry itself.

events.label: The Romantic backlash
events.description: Wordsworth, Coleridge, and the Romantics protested the machine's assault on nature and the human spirit. The argument over industry's soul began in the first generation.

events.label: Turner's 'Rain, Steam and Speed'
events.description: J. M. W. Turner painted a locomotive hurtling through storm and light, awe and dread together. Art was trying to see the new world the machine had made.

events.label: Zola's Germinal
events.description: Émile Zola's novel of a French miners' strike was the great Continental epic of industrial labor. The industrial novel had become a European form.

events.label: The Pullman Strike
events.description: A national railway boycott led by Eugene Debs was crushed by federal troops and injunctions. American industrial conflict had reached continental scale.

events.label: The Homestead Strike
events.description: A bloody battle between steelworkers and Pinkerton guards at Carnegie's Homestead works broke the union there. Industrial America's labor wars turned into pitched battles.

events.label: The Knights of Labor and the AFL
events.description: American workers built the Knights of Labor and then the craft-based AFL. The United States developed its own distinctive, often violent, labor movement.

events.label: Spencer and 'social Darwinism'
events.description: Herbert Spencer's 'survival of the fittest' was used to justify laissez-faire, inequality, and empire as natural law. Industrial competition acquired a pseudo-scientific defense.

events.label: Henry George's Progress and Poverty
events.description: Henry George asked why progress produced deeper poverty and proposed a single tax on land. It was one of the era's best-selling and most-debated economic books.

events.label: Bellamy's Looking Backward
events.description: Edward Bellamy's utopian novel imagined a peaceful socialist industrial future and inspired reform clubs across America. Industrial discontent fed a literature of alternatives.

events.label: Pomeranz and the 'California School'
events.description: Kenneth Pomeranz argued that China's Yangzi delta was as developed as Europe until coal and colonies tipped the balance. The claim reopened the entire 'why Europe' question.

events.label: Allen's high-wage-economy thesis
events.description: Robert Allen argued Britain industrialized because expensive labor and cheap coal made machines pay there first. It is now a leading, and contested, explanation.

events.label: The 'Industrious Revolution'
events.description: Households worked longer and bought more market goods before the factory, a demand-side run-up de Vries calls the 'industrious revolution.' Consumer desire may have preceded the machines.

events.label: Proto-industrialization
events.description: Rural cottage workers spun and wove for merchant capitalists under the 'putting-out' system before the factory. This dispersed industry was the seedbed the factory grew out of.

events.label: The great energy transition
events.description: Society shifted from the flow energy of muscle, wind, water, and wood to the vast stored energy of coal. This break with the biological energy ceiling is what made sustained growth possible.

events.label: The population explosion
events.description: Britain's population roughly tripled and Europe's doubled despite mass emigration. For the first time, more people did not mean Malthusian collapse.

events.label: Clock time and work discipline
events.description: The factory bell, the clock, and the fine for lateness imposed a new experience of time on human beings. How people lived inside a day was rebuilt around the machine.

events.label: The Great Stink of London
events.description: A summer of stench from the sewage-choked Thames finally forced Parliament to fund modern sewers. Sometimes reform required the river to reach the legislators' own windows.

events.label: William Wilberforce
events.description: The MP William Wilberforce led the parliamentary campaign that ended the British slave trade and then slavery itself. He showed organized conscience could move the industrial state.

events.label: Marx and Engels in Manchester and London
events.description: Engels' mill earnings funded the exiled Marx as they built scientific socialism from inside the workshop of the world. The system's deepest critics worked within its capital.

events.label: The coal–iron–rail–steam feedback loop
events.description: Railways needed iron and coal; mining and ironmaking needed railways and steam; each drove the others. This self-reinforcing loop was the engine of mid-century growth.

events.label: The electrification of the city
events.description: Electric light, trams, and power transformed work, leisure, and the night in the late-century city. The second wave reached ordinary daily life as the first never quite had.

events.label: Toward the assembly line
events.description: Meatpacking 'disassembly' lines and interchangeable parts converged on the moving assembly line Ford would perfect in 1913. The Industrial Revolution handed mass production to the twentieth century.

events.label: A divided world
events.description: By 1900 industrial nations were perhaps ten times richer per head than the societies they dominated. The Industrial Revolution's deepest legacy was global inequality.
